rows.Impact: Significantly impaired strength, recovery, and RIR accuracy; elevated injury risk
rows.Programming Response: Reduce volume 15-25%, avoid failure entirely, prioritize technique over intensity that session
Standard programming but avoid maximal pushes (no PR attempts)
Full capacity, good day for pushing progression

rows.Impact: Excellent (or may indicate under-recovery/illness if it's a sudden increase from baseline)
rows.Programming Response: Full capacity; if a sudden increase in sleep need appears, screen for overreaching/illness

rows.Guidance: 1.6-2.2g/kg bodyweight for most goals; 2.0-2.6g/kg during a fat-loss phase to protect muscle

Primary fuel for high-volume/high-intensity training; don't chronically under-fuel training days, especially in a deficit — carb timing around training can improve session quality and recovery perception
30-40ml/kg bodyweight/day as a general baseline, more with heavy sweat loss/heat

Ensure adequate intake, especially magnesium, zinc, vitamin D — deficiencies in these are common and can subtly impair recovery/sleep quality; whole-food-forward diet generally covers this without needing to prescribe supplements
Impairs sleep quality and protein synthesis; occasional moderate use is compatible with progress, frequent/heavy use is a common hidden cause of stalled recovery

rows.Description: Every 4-8 weeks depending on training age (beginners: 8-12wk; intermediate: 5-8wk; advanced: 4-6wk)
2+ consecutive sessions of unexpected strength/rep drop-off on multiple lifts
DOMS that doesn't resolve within normal timeframe (48-72h) or is worsening session-to-session
New or worsening joint discomfort not attributable to a single acute incident
Persistent low motivation, dread of training, irritability
Resting heart rate elevated, HRV depressed (if tracked), or subjectively poor sleep for 1+ week
Any active illness — always deload or fully rest, do not push through

rows.Best For: Most common, preserves strength/skill while reducing fatigue

rows.Protocol: Keep sets similar, reduce load ~40-50% (higher reps at lower RIR-demand)
rows.Best For: Good when joint/CNS fatigue is the primary issue

rows.Protocol: Reduce both volume and intensity moderately (~30% each)

rows.Best For: Illness, high injury risk signs, severe burnout

rows.Protocol: Light movement, mobility work, easy cardio, no structured lifting
rows.Best For: Mental burnout cases, in-season athletic contexts

rows.Warning Sign: Consistently feels 1-2 points higher than expected for the same load
Elevated 5+ bpm above baseline for several days
Restless, frequent waking, or needing significantly more sleep than usual

rows.Normal Range: Present, even if mild reluctance sometimes

rows.Warning Sign: Lasting 4+ days, or worsening across the week
Significant increase or decrease from baseline without other explanation

rows.Description: <6-7h sleep regularly, high stress, inconsistent nutrition
rows.Adjustment: Cap volume near MEV, avoid failure, deload every 4-5 weeks even for intermediates
Standard MEV-MAV programming, deload per standard schedule

rows.Description: 8h+ sleep, low stress, dialed nutrition, good training age
rows.Adjustment: Can approach MAV-MRV, slightly extend time between deloads, tolerate more intensity techniques

rows.Note: Generally excellent recovery capacity, but ensure sleep isn't sacrificed for school/social schedule — this is the most common limiter in this group
Highest general recovery capacity; standard rules apply
Still robust; slightly more attention to joint-specific recovery (tendon adaptation lags muscle adaptation)
Deload slightly more often (every 4-6 weeks even at intermediate level); prioritize protein and sleep consistency

Longer recovery windows between high-intensity sessions; deload every 3-5 weeks during higher-volume blocks; joint/tendon health work (light isometric/tendon loading) as ongoing "recovery investment," not just after injury

rows.Training Adjustment: Monitor; standard programming unless combined with poor sleep
Treat similarly to poor sleep — reduce volume/intensity 15-25%, protect adherence and basic movement over optimization, consider extra rest day if life stress is acute (e.g., major life event)

rows.Problem: Client resists taking a deload ("I don't want to lose progress")
rows.Fix: Reassure: a properly-timed deload typically results in a performance rebound (supercompensation) the following week, not a loss — frame as investment, not regression
Investigate non-training factors: sleep disorders, nutrition adequacy, life stress, medical issues — recommend professional evaluation if fatigue is disproportionate to training load

rows.Problem: Client deloads too often (every 2-3 weeks) out of anxiety
rows.Fix: Reframe: this prevents adequate volume accumulation for progress — set deloads on a fixed schedule (e.g., every 6 weeks) rather than reactively at the first sign of normal training fatigue
Insert an immediate deload or complete rest period (3-7 days), then resume at reduced volume (MEV) and ramp back up over 2-3 weeks
